name: Samael
description: Samael is a complex and controversial figure within Abrahamic traditions, particularly in Jewish mysticism and esoteric literature. He is often portrayed as an archangel, but one whose role is ambiguous, oscillating between divine servant and rebellious adversary. Some traditions identify him as the angel of death, wielding a sword and leading souls to the afterlife, while others associate him with temptation and the fall of humanity, even equating him with the serpent in the Garden of Eden. The etymology of his name, often interpreted as "venom of God" or "blindness of God," reflects this dual nature. He is neither wholly good nor entirely evil, but rather a powerful being operating within the divine plan, often serving as a tester or executioner of divine judgment. His association with Mars and fiery aspects further emphasizes his connection to strength, judgment, and potential destruction. Interpretations of Samael vary widely across different texts and schools of thought, reflecting the intricate and often contradictory nature of Abrahamic angelology.
subtitle: The Venom of God

keyMyths.title: Samael and the Fall of Adam and Eve
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, Samael is identified as the serpent who tempted Eve in the Garden of Eden. Driven by envy or a desire to test humanity's obedience, he allegedly convinced Eve to eat the forbidden fruit, leading to the fall of humankind. This interpretation positions Samael as a pivotal figure in the introduction of sin and mortality into the world, forever altering the relationship between humanity and the divine. His motives remain a subject of debate, ranging from malicious intent to fulfilling a necessary role in God's plan.
keyMyths.source: Ginzberg, Louis. *The Legends of the Jews*.

keyMyths.title: Samael as the Angel of Death
keyMyths.description: Samael is frequently depicted as the angel of death, responsible for taking souls to the afterlife. He is often visualized wielding a sword or possessing fiery attributes, reflecting the destructive and transformative nature of death. While a fearsome figure, his role is ultimately subservient to God's will, acting as an instrument of divine judgment and ensuring the proper transition of souls. This portrayal highlights the complex relationship between death, divine justice, and the ultimate fate of humanity.
keyMyths.source: Davidson, Gustav. *A Dictionary of Angels, Including the Fallen Angels*.

keyMyths.title: Samael and the Binding of Isaac
keyMyths.description: According to some midrashic traditions, Samael attempted to dissuade Abraham from sacrificing Isaac. He appeared in various guises, attempting to appeal to Abraham's emotions and questioning the morality of God's command. This narrative portrays Samael as a tempter, constantly challenging faith and obedience. Despite Samael's efforts, Abraham remained steadfast in his devotion, ultimately proving his unwavering commitment to God. This story reinforces the theme of testing and the importance of resisting temptation.
keyMyths.source: Aptowitzer, Victor. *The Documents of Jewish Sectaries*.

sources.author: Ginzberg, Louis
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Jewish folklore and midrashic traditions, offering diverse perspectives on Samael's role in various biblical narratives.
